title: The Curse of Durable Plastics in Museums
subtitle: 塑料文物的定时炸弹：博物馆如何抢救现代遗产

paragraphs.en: People often complain that plastics are too durable. Water bottles, shopping bags, and other trash litter the planet, from Mount Everest to the Mariana Trench, because plastics are everywhere and don't break down easily. But some plastic materials change over time. They crack and frizzle. They "weep" out additives. They melt into sludge. All of which creates huge headaches for institutions, such as museums, trying to preserve culturally important objects.
paragraphs.zh: 人们常常抱怨塑料过于耐用。从珠穆朗玛峰到马里亚纳海沟，水瓶、购物袋等垃圾遍布全球，因为塑料无处不在又不易降解。然而，有些塑料材质会随着时间推移而发生变化——开裂、卷曲、“渗出”添加剂、融化成污泥。这一切给博物馆等试图保护重要文化物品的机构带来了巨大的难题。

paragraphs.en: The variety of plastic objects at risk is dizzying: early radios, avant-garde sculptures, celluloid animation stills from Disney films, the first artificial heart. Certain artifacts are especially vulnerable because some pioneers in plastic art didn't always know how to mix ingredients properly, says Thea van Oosten, a polymer chemist who worked for decades at the Cultural Heritage Agency of the Netherlands. "It's like baking a cake: If you don't have exact amounts, it goes wrong," she says. "The object you make is already a time bomb."
paragraphs.zh: 面临风险的塑料制品种类之多令人目眩：早期收音机、前卫雕塑、迪士尼电影的赛璐珞动画胶片、第一颗人造心脏。曾在荷兰文化遗产局工作数十年的聚合物化学家西娅·范·奥斯滕指出，某些文物尤其脆弱，因为塑料艺术的先驱们并非总清楚如何精确配比原料。“这就像烤蛋糕：分量不对，整个就毁了。”她说，“你做出来的东西从一开始就是一颗定时炸弹。”

paragraphs.en: And sometimes, it's not the artist's fault. In the 1960s, the Italian artist Piero Gilardi began to create hundreds of bright, colorful foam pieces. Those pieces included small beds of roses and other items as well as a few dozen "nature carpets" — large rectangles decorated with foam pumpkins, cabbages, and watermelons. He wanted viewers to walk around on the carpets, which meant they had to be durable. Unfortunately, the polyurethane foam he used is inherently unstable.
paragraphs.zh: 而有时，问题并不在艺术家身上。20世纪60年代，意大利艺术家皮耶罗·吉拉迪开始创作数百件色彩鲜艳的泡沫作品，包括小型玫瑰花坛等物件，以及数十张“自然地毯”——用泡沫南瓜、卷心菜和西瓜装饰的大型矩形作品。他希望观众能在地毯上行走，这就要求作品足够耐用。遗憾的是，他使用的聚氨酯泡沫本身性质就不稳定。

paragraphs.en: It's especially vulnerable to light damage, and by the mid-1990s, Gilardi's pumpkins, roses, and other figures were splitting and crumbling. Museums locked some of them away in the dark. So van Oosten and her colleagues worked to preserve Gilardi's sculptures. They infused some with stabilizing and consolidating chemicals. Van Oosten calls those chemicals "sunscreens" because their goal was to prevent further light damage and rebuild worn polymer fibers. She is proud that several sculptures have even gone on display again, albeit sometimes beneath protective cases.
paragraphs.zh: 这种材料尤其容易受到光照损伤。到20世纪90年代中期，吉拉迪的南瓜、玫瑰和其他造型已开裂剥落。博物馆不得不将部分藏品封存于暗处。范·奥斯滕和同事们随后着手修复吉拉迪的雕塑，向其中注入起稳固和加固作用的化学物质。她将这些化学物质称为“防晒霜”，因为其目的是阻止光照继续造成损害，并重建磨损的聚合物纤维。她自豪地说，已有数件雕塑重新展出，尽管有时需要罩在保护壳下。

paragraphs.en: Despite success stories like van Oosten's, preservation of plastics will likely get harder. Old objects continue to deteriorate. Worse, biodegradable plastics designed to disintegrate, are increasingly common. And more is at stake here than individual objects. Joana Lia Ferreira, an assistant professor of conservation and restoration at the NOVA School of Science and Technology, notes that archaeologists first defined the great material ages of human history — Stone Age, Iron Age, and so on — after examining artifacts in museums.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管有范·奥斯滕这样的成功案例，塑料的保护工作未来可能愈发艰难。旧制品持续劣化，更糟的是，旨在快速降解的生物塑料正日益普及。况且，这里牵涉的远不止单件物品。NOVA科技大学保护与修复助理教授若安娜·莉亚·费雷拉指出，考古学家正是通过检视博物馆中的文物，才划定了人类历史上的伟大物质纪元——石器时代、铁器时代等等。

paragraphs.en: We now live in an age of plastic, she says, "and what we decide to collect today, what we decide to preserve...will have a strong impact on how in the future we'll be seen."
paragraphs.zh: 她说，我们如今身处塑料时代，“我们今天决定收藏什么、决定保存什么……将深刻影响未来的人们如何看待我们这个时代。”
